title: Multilingual Translation Models
concept: A single translation model can be trained to translate between many language pairs by prepending a target language token (e.g. '<2fr>') to the input.

exercises.prompt: What can a multilingual translation model do?
exercises.explain: A multilingual translation model can handle many different languages within a single model by using special target language tokens.

cards.title: One Model, Many Tongues
cards.body: Imagine having to download 50 different apps just to translate between 50 different languages. That would eat up all your phone's memory! What if one single AI model could translate any language to any other language using a simple trick?

cards.title: Multilingual Models
cards.body: Instead of training separate models for English-to-French, Spanish-to-German, and so on, a Multilingual Translation Model does it all. By adding a special token like '<2fr>' (meaning 'to French') to the beginning of your text, a single AI model instantly knows which language it needs to output.

cards.title: The Universal Translator
cards.body: Imagine a universal translator at an international space station. Instead of hiring different translators for every language pair, you have one super-translator. Before you whisper a message to them, you simply point to a flag on the wall (like the French flag 🇫🇷) to tell them what language they should output. The translator does the rest!

cards.title: Tokens in Action
cards.body:
Suppose you want to translate 'Hello, how are you?' into French and Spanish using the same model.

For French, you feed the model:
`'<2fr> Hello, how are you?'`
Output: `Bonjour, comment ça va ?`

For Spanish, you feed it:
`'<2es> Hello, how are you?'`
Output: `Hola, ¿cómo estás?`

cards.title: Myth: One Model Per Pair
cards.body: People often think you need a brand-new model for every new language pair you want to translate. In reality, modern AI models share knowledge across languages! Learning Spanish actually helps the model understand French grammar better, making the single multilingual model incredibly powerful and efficient.

cards.title: Visualizing Multilingual Setup
cards.body: Instead of managing separate pipelines for every language, we use a single model and guide it with a simple prefix token.

left.text: Separate models for English-to-French, English-to-Spanish, and French-to-Spanish. Lots of storage needed!

right.text: One single model. Just change the starting token ('<2fr>' or '<2es>') to get the language you want!

exercises.prompt: Why do we add a token like '<2es>' at the beginning of the text in a multilingual model?

options.text: It makes the translation speed twice as slow.
It tells the model which language to translate the text into.
It deletes all the punctuation from the output.
It automatically translates the text into every language at once.

exercises.explain: Adding a target language token (like '<2es>') at the start of the input tells the single AI model which language to output.

exercises.prompt: True or False: We prepend the target language token to the input text to guide the translation output.
exercises.explain: Yes! The special token is prepended (placed at the start) so the model knows the target language before generating the translation.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete how we control the output language of a multilingual model.
exercises.sentence: To set the target language, we prepend a special target language _ to our input text.
exercises.explain: By prepending a target language token (like <2de> for German) to the input, we guide the multilingual model to output the correct translation.

exercises.prompt: Which option demonstrates a multilingual translation model using a target language token?
exercises.explain: AI model B successfully uses the target language token (<2fr>) prepended to the text to produce a French translation. Model A ignores the instructions, and Model C represents old-fashioned word-by-word translation.

exercises.prompt: You want to use a multilingual model to translate 'Good morning' into Spanish. How do you tell the model your target language?

options.text: Add a footnote at the end of the text.
Prepend a target language token like '<2es>' to the front of the input.
Train a completely new model for each sentence.
Translate the sentence word-by-word using a dictionary.

exercises.explain: To tell a single translation model which language you want, you prepend a target language token (like '<2es>' for Spanish) to the very beginning of the input text. This guides the model to translate to the correct target language.
